title: Topic 220: Why Escort Missions Are Universally Hated: Bad Design Psychology
content:
## Topic 220: Why Escort Missions Are Universally Hated: Bad Design Psychology

### ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the near-universal disdain for escort missions within the gaming community, a phenomenon as reliable as gravity and twice as frustrating. Through a rigorous, albeit humorous, analysis of game design, player psychology, and the sheer, mind-numbing stupidity of non-player characters (NPCs), we will demonstrate that the escort mission is not merely a flawed mechanic, but a masterclass in psychological torment. By examining the psychological principles of frustration, loss of agency, and the uncanny valley of artificial intelligence, this paper will prove that the hatred of escort missions is not just a matter of opinion, but a quantifiable, predictable, and entirely justified reaction to bad design. The implications of this study extend beyond the digital realm, offering a cautionary tale for any service designer who has ever considered making their customers feel like glorified, unpaid babysitters.

### INTRODUCTION

The year is 1997. You are James Bond, the world’s most famous secret agent. You have a license to kill, a Walther PPK, and a seemingly endless supply of perfectly tailored tuxedos. You have disarmed nuclear warheads, seduced countless women, and saved the world more times than you can count. And now, you are faced with your most daunting challenge yet: escorting Natalya Simonova through the treacherous streets of St. Petersburg. She moves at the speed of a tectonic plate, possesses the self-preservation instincts of a lemming, and has a unique talent for finding the exact spot on the map where every single enemy has a clear line of fire. This, dear reader, is the escort mission in its purest, most infuriating form.

For decades, game developers have subjected players to these digital trials of patience, seemingly oblivious to the torrent of rage they inspire. From the suicidal scientists of *Half-Life* to the perpetually endangered Ashley Graham in *Resident Evil 4*, the escort mission has become a universally recognized symbol of bad game design. But why? What is it about these seemingly innocuous tasks that transforms even the most mild-mannered gamer into a frothing, controller-throwing berserker? This paper will argue that the answer lies not in the difficulty of the missions themselves, but in their fundamental disregard for the psychological needs of the player. We will explore how escort missions systematically violate the principles of good game design, creating a perfect storm of frustration, helplessness, and a burning desire to see the NPC you are sworn to protect walk into a landmine. By the end of this paper, you will not only understand why you hate escort missions, but you will also have a newfound appreciation for the delicate art of not making your players want to tear their hair out.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

While the hallowed halls of academia have yet to produce a definitive, peer-reviewed study on the psychological impact of escort missions (a shocking oversight, we contend), a wealth of anecdotal evidence and proto-academic analysis can be found in the digital trenches of the internet. Gaming forums, blogs, and video essays have long served as a de facto support group for players traumatized by their experiences as digital bodyguards. A recurring theme in these discussions is the concept of “loss of agency.” As one astute observer on Reddit noted, “The player is not able to progress at their own pace; they must wait for the NPC.” [1] This sentiment is echoed in a 2016 article on Gamasutra, which identifies the loss of control as a key reason for player frustration. [2]

The psychological underpinnings of this frustration are well-documented, if not directly applied to escort missions. The frustration-aggression hypothesis, first proposed by Dollard, Doob, Miller, Mowrer, and Sears in 1939, posits that frustration—the blocking of a goal-directed behavior—inevitably leads to aggression. [3] In the context of an escort mission, the player’s goal (to complete the level) is constantly thwarted by the NPC’s incompetence, leading to a predictable and entirely understandable surge of aggressive feelings. Furthermore, the work of Deci and Ryan on Self-Determination Theory highlights the importance of autonomy, competence, and relatedness in human motivation. [4] Escort missions, by their very nature, undermine all three of these psychological needs. The player’s autonomy is stripped away, their competence is rendered irrelevant by the NPC’s suicidal tendencies, and their relatedness to the character is often one of pure, unadulterated loathing.

### ANALYSIS

The psychological torture of the escort mission can be broken down into three key components: the crippling loss of agency, the infuriating incompetence of the AI, and the cognitive dissonance of being a powerful hero reduced to a glorified nanny.

**The Loss of Agency:** At its core, the appeal of video games lies in the player’s ability to exert control over a digital world. We are the masters of our own destiny, the captains of our own starships, the plumbers who can jump really, really high. Escort missions shatter this illusion of control. Suddenly, our progress is tethered to a character who moves at a glacial pace, stops for no apparent reason, and seems to have a magnetic attraction to enemy bullets. We are no longer the protagonist of our own story, but a side character in someone else’s, and a deeply frustrating one at that. This loss of agency is a direct violation of the player’s psychological contract with the game, a betrayal that breeds resentment and a desperate desire to abandon the NPC to their fate.

**The Uncanny Valley of Stupidity:** The NPCs in escort missions are a marvel of artificial incompetence. They are programmed with just enough intelligence to navigate the game world, but not enough to avoid walking directly into a room full of heavily armed enemies. They will stand in the open during a firefight, run in circles when they should be taking cover, and generally behave in a manner that suggests they have the survival instincts of a dodo. This is not simply a matter of bad programming; it is a fundamental design flaw. As noted in the Gamasutra article, “designing a complex AI partner is usually not in the budget.” [2] The result is a character who is just smart enough to be annoying, but not smart enough to be helpful, a digital golem of pure, unadulterated frustration.

**The Hero as Babysitter:** The final nail in the coffin of the escort mission is the cognitive dissonance it creates. We are playing as a hero, a warrior, a savior of the galaxy. We have slain dragons, toppled empires, and single-handedly repelled alien invasions. And now, we are being asked to babysit a character who would likely be defeated by a stiff breeze. This is not the power fantasy we signed up for. It is a tedious, demeaning, and utterly unheroic task that undermines our sense of accomplishment and makes us question our life choices. The escort mission transforms the hero’s journey into a tedious errand, a noble quest into a frustrating chore.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

No discussion of escort missions would be complete without a rogues’ gallery of the most infamous offenders. These are the missions that have been seared into the collective memory of the gaming community, the ones that still haunt our nightmares.

***Resident Evil 4* - Ashley Graham:** The president’s daughter is a master of getting herself kidnapped. She is a damsel in distress on an industrial scale, a one-woman argument for the abolition of the escort mission. Her shrill cries of “Leon!” have become a Pavlovian trigger for a generation of gamers, a sound that will forever be associated with frustration, panic, and a deep, abiding hatred for the color yellow.

***GoldenEye 007* - Natalya Simonova:** As mentioned in the introduction, Natalya is the platonic ideal of the terrible escort mission NPC. She is slow, she is stupid, and she has a knack for getting herself killed in the most inconvenient ways possible. The mission to escort her through the streets of St. Petersburg is a masterclass in frustration, a digital crucible designed to test the limits of human patience.

***Half-Life* - The Scientists:** The scientists of Black Mesa are a testament to the fact that a high IQ does not translate to common sense. These bespectacled geniuses will happily wander into a nest of headcrabs, stand in a pool of radioactive waste, or simply refuse to follow you for no discernible reason. They are a liability in a lab coat, a walking, talking, and frequently dying argument for the importance of natural selection.

### DISCUSSION

The universal hatred of escort missions offers a fascinating insight into the psychology of consumer behavior. At its core, the frustration we experience with these missions is a microcosm of the frustration we feel when a product or service fails to meet our expectations. We expect a game to be fun, engaging, and empowering. When it is instead tedious, frustrating, and disempowering, we feel cheated. This is the same feeling we get when we are stuck on hold with customer service for an hour, when a product breaks the day after the warranty expires, or when a website is so poorly designed that we can’t find the information we are looking for.

In all of these cases, the root of our frustration is a sense of powerlessness. We are at the mercy of a system that is indifferent to our needs, a system that seems designed to thwart us at every turn. The escort mission is a perfect metaphor for this experience. It is a system that is ostensibly designed to help us, but in reality, only serves to make our lives more difficult. The lesson for businesses is clear: do not make your customers feel like they are on an escort mission. Do not make them feel powerless, frustrated, and unheard. Empower them, respect their time, and for the love of all that is holy, do not make them babysit your incompetent AI.

### CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the escort mission is a deeply flawed and universally despised game mechanic that serves as a cautionary tale for designers in all fields. It is a masterclass in how to frustrate, disempower, and enrage your audience. By violating the fundamental psychological needs of the player, the escort mission creates an experience that is not just challenging, but actively unpleasant. It is a testament to the resilience of the human spirit that we have endured these digital trials for so long.

Perhaps one day, a game developer will finally crack the code and create an escort mission that is actually fun. Perhaps one day, we will be able to protect an NPC without wanting to strangle them with our controller cord. But until that day comes, we will continue to dread the words “escort mission,” and we will continue to share our stories of digital babysitting with a mixture of horror and humor. And who knows, maybe one day, the hallowed halls of academia will finally give this topic the attention it so richly deserves.
references:
[1] Reddit. (2017). *Everything bad about Escort Missions -- Reversed*. Retrieved from https://www.reddit.com/r/gamedesign/comments/639us1/everything_bad_about_escort_missions_reversed/
[2] Bycer, J. (2016). *Can we Fix Escort Mission Game Design?* Game Developer. Retrieved from https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/can-we-fix-escort-mission-game-design-
[3] Dollard, J., Doob, L. W., Miller, N. E., Mowrer, O. H., & Sears, R. R. (1939). *Frustration and aggression*. Yale University Press.
[4] Ryan, R. M., & Deci, E. L. (2000). Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being. *American Psychologist, 55*(1), 68–78.
